payload.text:
Build a high-quality, production-ready web application called “IRON QUEST – Home Workout RPG Tracker”, a gamified home-workout tracking platform that combines RPG mechanics, fitness progression, and AI coaching. The application must be visually stunning, modern, and immersive, using a dark RPG-inspired UI with smooth animations, card-based layouts, progress bars, level-up effects, and subtle sound/visual feedback. The core concept is that the user is a character in an RPG world who levels up their real-life body by completing workouts. The app must be built using HTML, modern CSS (Flexbox/Grid), and JavaScript, fully client-side, with all data persisted using localStorage (auto-save on every interaction, usable offline, no login required). The main layout uses a left sidebar navigation with icons and labels (Dashboard, Character, Quests, Custom Workout, AI Workout Generator, Coach Assistant, Progress, Inventory, Calendar, Settings) and a main content area with animated cards and panels.

The Dashboard must immediately show the character avatar (stylized silhouette), current Level, EXP bar (green glowing), current streak (🔥), today’s quest status, and a motivational RPG-style message. The Character page must display RPG stats such as Strength (push-ups), Endurance (plank/time-based workouts), Mobility (squats), and Consistency (streak), each with bars and numeric values derived from actual completed workouts. Leveling up unlocks new workout types and visual feedback such as glowing borders, level-up popups, and text-based “lore” messages.

The Quest system represents predefined daily workouts (Beginner to Advanced), but the app must also include a Custom Workout System as a core mandatory feature. In the Custom Workout page, users must be required to create at least 5 workout entries before starting a workout session. Each custom workout input form includes: Workout Name (text), Type selector (Reps or Minutes), Reps or Minutes value (number), Sets (number). If the workout type is Minutes, the system must automatically provide a built-in timer with Start/Pause/Reset controls, visual countdown animation, and completion detection. Only after a minimum of 5 workouts are added can the “Start Workout Session” button unlock. During a session, workouts are shown sequentially with clear progress indicators, set counters, and completion animations.

Add an AI Workout Generator feature powered by GEMINI AI. This feature appears as a guided form where users input: Age, Body Weight, Maximum Push-ups they can currently do, Primary Goal (Lose Weight / Build Muscle), and optional notes. After submission, GEMINI AI must generate a complete beginner-appropriate workout plan, automatically filling the Custom Workout list (minimum 5 workouts), including reps/minutes and sets, adjusted to the user’s ability level. The generated workouts must be editable before starting, and clearly labeled as “AI Generated Plan.”

Include a dedicated Coach Assistant page powered by GEMINI AI, presented as a friendly RPG-style coach character. This AI Coach must be able to: answer workout questions, suggest what to train today, motivate the user when streaks break, explain exercises in simple language, and adapt responses based on the user’s stored stats and progress. The Coach Assistant UI should resemble an in-game dialogue system with chat bubbles, typing animation, quick-reply buttons (e.g., “What should I train today?”, “I feel tired”, “How to get stronger push-ups”), and contextual awareness of the user’s level and history.

The Progress & Stats page must include charts and logs showing total workouts completed, max push-ups achieved, total workout time, body weight history (manual input), and notes/mood per day. The Calendar & Streak page must visually display workout days (green), rest days (gray), and missed days (red), with a prominent streak counter and gentle narrative feedback when streaks are broken. The Inventory & Badge system must reward users with badges, titles, and visual achievements for milestones such as first workout, first push-up, 7-day streak, 30 workouts completed, etc., displayed in a grid with locked/unlocked states.

All data—character level, EXP, custom workouts, AI-generated workouts, stats, calendar, inventory, settings—must be saved automatically to localStorage. The Settings page must include theme toggle (dark/light), beginner mode toggle, reset progress with confirmation, and export/import progress as JSON. The overall tone of the app must be supportive, game-like, and non-judgmental, making the user feel like they are progressing through an RPG journey rather than following a strict fitness program. The final result should feel like a polished indie fitness game, not a basic tracker, with strong UX, clean typography, responsive design, and clear visual hierarchy throughout.

**Defining the Application's Focus**

I'm now zeroing in on defining the application's focus. The plan is an RPG-themed home workout tracker, aptly named "IRON QUEST," leveraging React, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS for its SPA structure. I'm prioritizing RPG mechanics like XP, levels, and stats (Strength, Endurance).


**Outlining Feature Scope**

Now I'm diving deep into the application's structure. I have a clearer outline of the user interface. It will feature a sidebar navigation with components for Dashboard, Character, Quests, Custom Workout, AI Generator, Coach, Progress, Inventory, Calendar, and Settings. I'm focusing on defining each component, like the Dashboard's avatar, level, XP bar, and streak. I'm also planning the specifics of AI integration via Gemini, including a workout generator and coaching assistant.


**Refining Application Architecture**

I'm adapting my approach to routing; a state-based view switcher provides control for the "game loop" feel. I've begun to define the file structure: `types.ts`, `constants.ts`, and component organization. The plan is to have a folder for reusable UI elements. The `services/` will wrap Gemini API calls and local storage functions. Also, I'm thinking of a Dark Mode theme, with neon accents.


**Defining Data & Structure**

I'm now concretely defining the data model for local storage. I've sketched out the `userProfile`, `workoutHistory`, `customRoutine`, `inventory`, and `settings` objects. The structure is key, so that it will reflect RPG elements (XP, Mana vibes). Also, I've selected a system font like Inter. Lastly, I'm forming a step-by-step implementation plan.


**Integrating Components Systematically**

I'm now integrating the components systematically, with `App.tsx` handling state management to tie everything together. The workout player, or `ActiveSession`, and `AIGenerator`, are core to the application. I'm focusing on the integration of Gemini, with refined prompts now using JSON schema for the generator and a contextual persona for the coach. Finally, I'm addressing constraints like the built-in timer, minimum exercise requirements, and local storage persistence.


**Structuring the Data Flow**

Now, I'm defining the data flow: user workout, which triggers `completeSession()`, updating XP, stats, and workout history. I'll need to check for level-ups and badges and save the updated data. I'm addressing the active workout session, deciding between a modal and a dedicated full-screen view. I'm also addressing the API key via process.env. Finally, I'm working with the proper use of dependency arrays to prevent infinite loops.


**Refining Application's Aesthetics**

I'm now focusing on aesthetics to create a strong RPG vibe. I'm prioritizing borders, glowing effects, and animations using standard Tailwind classes to avoid unnecessary dependencies. I'm also addressing accessibility concerns, particularly with contrast and proper labeling. I'm also confirming the file structure: `index.tsx`, `index.html`, `App.tsx`, `types.ts`, `services/geminiService.ts`, `services/storageService.ts`, and core components are as planned.

Application entry point
TypeScript definitions for the application
LocalStorage persistence service
Gemini API integration for Workout Generator and Coach
Navigation sidebar component
Shared UI components like Cards and Progress Bars
Dashboard View Component

payload.text:
**Troubleshooting 'Sword'**

I'm currently focused on resolving a "Cannot find name 'Sword'" compilation error within `views/Dashboard.tsx`, specifically at line 101. It looks like 'Sword' is not properly imported or defined within the scope of this file. My next step will be to examine the imports and the broader codebase to determine the correct import path or declaration.


**Examining 'Sword' Import**

I've shifted my focus to the import statements within `Dashboard.tsx`. I see that `Sword` isn't imported from any of the standard components like `ProgressBar` or `Button`. I'm going to look closer at the `lucide-react` import to ensure the icon is available, as I am trying to resolve the "Cannot find name 'Sword'" compilation error. Then, I plan to search the project for other usages of 'Sword' to see where the component is originally defined.


**Adding 'Sword' Import**

I've zeroed in on the core issue. The "Cannot find name 'Sword'" error stems from a missing import statement in `Dashboard.tsx`. I've updated the imports to include `Sword` from `lucide-react`, resolving this compilation error. Specifically, I've appended `Sword` to the existing import list. I'm now verifying that this resolves the error within the file, and that no other files need an update.


**Verifying Chart Implementation**

I'm now implementing a `LineChart` from `recharts` to render workout history data within the `Dashboard.tsx` file. I've integrated `LineChart`, `Line`, `XAxis`, `YAxis`, `Tooltip`, and `ResponsiveContainer` and I'm using an array mapping to render workout durations. The chart seems to be rendering properly so far.


**Confirming React Components**

I'm now implementing the `Sword` import to `Dashboard.tsx`, confirming that the icon is correctly being pulled from `lucide-react`. I am checking the rest of the file to see if there are any other errors as well. Finally, I will output the new updated XML file for `Dashboard.tsx`.


**Validating Prop Passing**

I'm now verifying the prop passing in `App.tsx` and `Dashboard.tsx`. It looks like props for `ActiveSession`, `CustomWorkout`, and `AIGenerator` are being passed correctly in `App.tsx`. The core issue still seems to be the missing import of `Sword` in `Dashboard.tsx`, as I resolved that issue previously. The `recharts` usage in `App.tsx` seems fine; I'll assume the environment has the necessary dependencies since no error reported, and I should only touch the code with an error. I am making sure not to add any new files or dependencies.


**Refining Component Usage**

I've confirmed that adding the `Sword` import from `lucide-react` to `Dashboard.tsx` resolves the original compilation error. I've also verified the integration of other components like `Card`, `ProgressBar`, and `Button` and now I'm making sure that the props are being passed correctly. I have now looked at the component and the dependencies and verified it is all correct.


**Implementing 'Shield' Icon**

I'm now integrating the `Shield` icon within the `Dashboard.tsx` component. It's being wrapped in a div with styling to achieve a specific visual appearance, including a rounded background, border, and shadow. I'm also ensuring the user level is correctly displayed as an overlay.


**Integrating XP Visual**

I'm now focusing on visually representing the user's XP progress within `Dashboard.tsx`, integrating a progress bar and the current/maximum XP display. I've successfully added the HTML structure, including the div elements and the use of inline styles for the progress bar width, driven by `xpPercentage`.  I've added animation effects for a visual progress.


**Adding 'Activity' Icon**

I'm now integrating the `Activity` icon within the `Dashboard.tsx` component. This icon is being added within the section that displays workout statistics, which displays the total number of workouts and the total time. I've now integrated the new icon with the rest of the file.


**Adding Trophy Icon**

I'm now integrating the `Trophy` icon within the `Dashboard.tsx` component, in the user stats section. I've placed the icon within a styled div, similar to how I handled the other icons previously. I'm focusing on ensuring it aligns with the existing layout and styling of the component.


**Generating XML File**

I'm now generating the updated XML file for `Dashboard.tsx`, incorporating all recent changes, including the integration of icons and XP visualization. I've double-checked all elements to ensure accurate rendering and placement. I'll also verify that `App.tsx` has everything it needs.


**Analyzing Component Integration**

I'm now focusing on integrating all the icons, as the original compilation issue with the Sword icon has been resolved, but I am now focused on integrating new icons such as the `Shield`, `Activity`, and `Trophy` icons. I am also checking the other components such as `recharts` for the line chart implementation. I've confirmed that the logic in `PROGRESS` view is correct, which seems fine for `recharts`. I am also checking `App.tsx` imports to make sure there are no issues.


**Refining Visual Representation**

I'm now refining the visual representation of XP progress within `Dashboard.tsx`. I've recently adjusted the inline styles for the progress bar width, linking the value to the `xpPercentage`. I've added animation effects for a visual progress. I've also now added a check for the user's level. I am also currently adding the new icon, Trophy.


**Validating Level Data**

I'm now verifying the display of user level and XP data within the `Dashboard.tsx` component. I'm confirming that the `user.level`, `user.currentXp`, and `user.maxXp` values are correctly pulled and displayed. I've focused on ensuring the progress bar correctly visualizes the XP percentage using `xpPercentage`, including animation for a better user experience.


**Integrating 'Flame' Icon**

I'm now integrating the `Flame` icon within the `Dashboard.tsx` component. I'm focusing on adding the flame icon into a card, using the `animate-pulse-slow` animation effect. I've placed the icon within a styled div, similar to how I handled the other icons previously. I'm also now adding the new `Activity` icon.



**Adding 'Zap' Icon**

I'm now integrating the `Zap` icon within the `Dashboard.tsx` component. This icon is being added within the section that displays workout statistics, which displays the total number of workouts and the total time. I've now integrated the new icon with the rest of the file.


**Verifying Quest Completion**

I'm now focusing on the 'Today's Quest' section within `Dashboard.tsx`, specifically verifying the conditional styling and icon display based on `dailyQuest.completed`. I've added a check for the conditional, to ensure that it correctly renders and is able to complete the new functionality, with the Sword component. I'm confirming that the Sword icon's appearance and the surrounding styling change when the quest is marked as completed, updating the file.
<!-- Fixed: Import Sword icon from lucide-react to resolve the undefined variable error in Dashboard.tsx. -->

entries.description: Import Sword icon from lucide-react to fix compilation error.

payload.text:
Integrate exercise explanations and video demonstrations into the IRON QUEST app. For each exercise available in the Quests, Custom Workouts, and AI Workout Generator, include a brief, easy-to-understand text description of the correct form and a link to a short, high-quality video tutorial. This should be accessible directly from the workout interface and the Custom Workout creation page to ensure users are performing exercises safely and effectively.

Extend the IRON QUEST application with a fully integrated Exercise Education System to ensure safety, correctness, and confidence for all users, especially beginners. For every exercise that appears in Quests, Custom Workouts, and AI-generated workout plans, include an Exercise Info Module that can be accessed directly from the workout card, exercise list, or creation form. Each exercise must have a short, clear, beginner-friendly text explanation describing the correct form, key movement cues, breathing tips, and common mistakes to avoid. Alongside the text explanation, include a link or embedded preview to a short, high-quality video demonstration sourced from reputable platforms (e.g., YouTube tutorials by certified trainers or well-known fitness channels). The video link must open in a clean modal or side panel without navigating away from the app. In the Custom Workout creation page, when a user inputs or selects an exercise name, the UI must immediately offer an “Exercise Info” button that previews the explanation and video so users understand how to perform the movement before starting the workout.

The overall visual style of the application must avoid medieval or fantasy aesthetics. Instead, enforce a modern, tech-forward, minimalist RPG-inspired UI. The design language should feel like a modern productivity app mixed with light gamification, not a medieval fantasy game. Use clean typography, flat or soft-gradient surfaces, subtle shadows, rounded corners, and restrained animations. Replace medieval metaphors (scrolls, swords, parchment textures) with modern equivalents such as progress dashboards, sleek cards, neon-accent highlights, and data-driven visuals. The RPG feeling should come from progression systems, levels, and stats, not from medieval visuals.

Add a Landing / Onboarding Screen as the first entry point of the app. This page should function as a clean, focused setup experience rather than a marketing-heavy landing page. The Landing Screen must guide users through profile creation and personalization only, with no distractions. Include a step-based onboarding flow that collects essential profile data: Username or Display Name, Age, Height, Body Weight, Fitness Goal (Lose Weight / Build Muscle / Stay Active), Current Ability Level (including “How many push-ups can you do?”), and optional notes or limitations. The UI should feel calm, modern, and encouraging, using progress indicators (step 1 of 3, step 2 of 3, etc.) and inline validation. Upon completion, the profile data must be saved to localStorage and used throughout the app to personalize workouts, AI suggestions, stats scaling, and Coach Assistant responses.

Implement a dedicated Profile Page accessible from the sidebar. This page must display the user’s avatar, name, level, core stats, goals, and historical body metrics. Users must be able to edit profile information at any time, update body weight, adjust goals, and reset or recalibrate their difficulty level. Profile data must directly influence AI Workout Generator outputs, daily quest difficulty, and Coach Assistant advice. The Profile UI should be clean and dashboard-like, using cards and sections instead of long forms, reinforcing a modern application feel.

All new features must maintain the same standards of UI quality, consistency, responsiveness, and auto-save behavior as the rest of the application. The Exercise Education system, modernized UI direction, Landing Screen, and Profile system should feel like core parts of a polished, real-world product, not add-ons.

**Developing Exercise Education**

I'm currently focused on the "IRON QUEST" application, specifically the exercise education system. I plan to incorporate textual descriptions and video links for each exercise. I'm aiming to make this information accessible across various parts of the app: from Quests, to Custom Workouts, and even the AI Generator. This integration should create a cohesive learning experience for users.


**Implementing Exercise Integration**

I've added an "Exercise Info" button to the Custom Workout creation page. When clicked, it will open the exercise video within a modal or side panel, enhancing the user experience.


**Refining Exercise Data**

I'm now implementing the exercise information system. I'm adding `height`, `goal`, `abilityLevel`, and `notes` fields. I'm also considering an `ExerciseInfo` type or an extension of the `Exercise` type to include description and video URL. Since custom exercises are user-defined, I'll incorporate a predefined library and a "Search Video" link for the unknown exercises. This will be triggered within a selection or lookup, and implemented via an `EXERCISE_DATABASE` const. Finally, I will also be updating the fonts in the application to meet the demands of a more "modern, tech-forward" user interface. I'm looking into using "Inter" for font consistency.


**Refining App Aesthetics**

I'm now focusing on a visual overhaul. I'm leaning toward using 'Inter' for body text and 'Orbitron' for headers and the logo to create a sci-fi RPG vibe. I'll update the Tailwind config with a neon-accent, dark-mode-friendly color palette. I plan to revamp the `Sidebar.tsx`, changing icons to be less medieval (like swapping `Sword` for `Target` or `Zap`) and updating the visual style. I'll also modernize `Card` and `Button` styles in `Shared.tsx`. Finally, I'll build a multi-step onboarding form in `Onboarding.tsx` and save user profiles to state/localstorage. I'll also update the profile page in `Profile.tsx`.


**Modernizing Interface Components**

I'm now diving into modernizing the user interface. I'm focusing on the dashboard and profile pages. I'll refactor the "Character" page, now named "Profile.tsx", to include editing capabilities. I'm working to integrate "Exercise Info" feature into the custom workout page. I'm also planning to create an exercise database in `data/exercises.ts` to show the information button when a user types a known exercise. I'll add a modal for video display. I will also be updating the App.tsx to handle the Onboarding state. If a user lacks profile information, they'll be directed to the onboarding process. Lastly, I'm refactoring `services/gemini.ts` to utilize the new user profile data.


**Prioritizing Aesthetic Overhaul**

I'm now focusing on the app's visual identity, starting with the `index.html` to integrate new fonts and colors. I'm prioritizing `Cinzel` font for the RPG feel, while also introducing a new aesthetic. I'll then move onto updating types, and UI components with a new color scheme. I'll ensure to maintain the RPG theme while modernizing the design.


**Constructing Exercise Database**

I'm now building the exercise database with placeholder video links. I'm focusing on providing generic YouTube search links or specific, reliable video IDs where available, like for Push-ups (`IODxDxX7oi4`), Squats (`YaXPRqUwItQ`), and Plank (`pSHjTRCQxIw`). I'll create an `ExerciseInfoModal` helper component to manage video display, using a reliable YouTube embed format via iframe for previews.


**Refining Style Implementation**

I'm now refining the style implementation and considering replacing `Cinzel` with `'Exo 2'` for a more tech-forward aesthetic. I'm also changing colors to `cyan-500` and `violet-500`. I will proceed to integrate changes across various files, including adding `height`, `goal`, `abilityLevel`, and `notes` to the `UserProfile` type, creating the `ExerciseModal` component, and updating the UI of the dashboard, sidebar, and custom workout views. I'll be updating the `AIGenerator.tsx` to use profile data, and I'll modify `services/gemini.ts` to update the prompts to match. I'm also ensuring the overall functionality is connected in `App.tsx`. I'll be replacing the character data with new profile, with either a file overwrite or renaming approach.

entries.description: Update font family to Exo 2 and color palette for modern tech-RPG aesthetic
Update UserProfile with new fields and add ExerciseInfo type
Database of exercises with descriptions and video links
Modal component for displaying exercise info and video
Update UI components for modern tech look
Update sidebar icons and styling
New Onboarding view for profile setup
New Profile/Character view with editing stats
Update CustomWorkout to include Exercise Info modal integration
Update Dashboard to modern tech visual style

payload.text:
Buat pilihan Bahasa, English / Indonesian (Buat bahasa Indonesia santai) Sama untuk pemutar video di youtube (embed) TIDAK BISA DI PUTAR SAMA SEKALI errorrnya
Tonton video di YouTube
Error 153
Terjadi error pada konfigurasi pemutar video
Kalau bisa tolong fix dan tolong banget agar embed youtube / tutorial gerakannya bisa di tunjukkan ketika workout di samping counter, tambahin animasi yang BANYAK sekali pas workout supaya seru

**Considering Application Upgrades**

I'm currently focused on the IRON QUEST application enhancements. My priorities are clear: implement a language toggle – English and a more casual Indonesian – and resolve the YouTube embed error (Error 153). I'm planning out the architecture for both.


**Addressing YouTube Embeds**

I'm now fully focused on fixing the YouTube embed error and integrating the workout experience. My plan involves verifying the iframe construction, and video URL format, and I'm looking into referrer policies or domain restrictions. I'm also planning to display the tutorial video alongside the counter. I will add animations to the active workout session.


**Implementing Video and Language**

I'm now diving into the implementation details. I'll focus on replacing restricted video IDs and making the iframe properties more permissive to fix the YouTube embed issue. I'll split the active session screen to showcase the video and timer, and also add animations, focusing on making the experience engaging. Finally, I will integrate the language toggle with a `Language` type, and lookups for the internationalization.


**Refining Video Embed**

I'm now zeroing in on the video embed. I'll switch the URL to `youtube-nocookie.com` and meticulously check the `allow` attributes, along with including `referrerPolicy="no-referrer"` to bypass potential local restrictions. I'm also planning the active session animations: a background pulse, progress bar glow, "Combo" or "XP" pop-ups, and confetti on set completion. Then, I will address the language toggle, and add `language: 'en' | 'id'` to the `UserProfile` type. I'm leaning toward a separate translation object for neatness, possibly in a `Shared.tsx` component.


**Implementing i18n and Storage**

I'm now adding `utils/i18n.ts` and will try to keep the App component and use either a context, or a `lang` prop to minimize overhead. I'll modify `types.ts` to include the dictionary type and `services/storage.ts` to save the selected language, as well.

**Adding Indonesian Translation**

I'm updating `data/exercises.ts` with Indonesian descriptions, and I'll modify the UI strings in `Dashboard`, `Sidebar`, and the other components to support the new language.

**Replacing YouTube IDs**

I'm replacing video IDs in `data/exercises.ts` with generic public fitness tutorials to address the "This video is not available" error.

**Adding Active Session CSS**

I'll add a CSS animation for "pumping" or "glowing" in `ActiveSession.tsx` to add visual feedback during the workout.


**Integrating Language Options**

I'm now expanding the user profile to include language selection, and I will also update the exercise data with Indonesian descriptions. I'll modify the storage service to reflect a default language of 'en'. The sidebar will now have a language toggle, using a flag icon for ease of use. I'm also preparing the active session view with a layout for video and controls, along with CSS animations, and logic to autostart the video. Finally, I will apply translations to the dashboard, custom workout, and onboarding views and refine the translation strategy via a `data/translations.ts` file.


**Refining Workout Enhancements**

I'm now implementing the latest updates. I will try to address the YouTube issues by ensuring the IDs are valid and will experiment with standard IDs for exercises. I'm focusing on cleaning up the embed and incorporating `rel=0` and `modestbranding=1`. I'll also add a background gradient shift, 'animate-ping' for the timer, and 'animate-bounce' for completion to add feedback during the active session. Finally, I will integrate the changes.


**Prioritizing Code Updates**

I'm now zeroing in on the file updates. I'll focus on the core files: `types.ts` for language support; `data/exercises.ts` for video fixes and Indonesian text; `services/storage.ts` to initialize the language; and `App.tsx` for state management and language selection. I'll maintain simplicity by not using a shared component.


**Implementing ActiveSession and Translations**

I'm now integrating the `useLanguage` hook and dictionary from `data/translations.ts`. I will update the sidebar for language selection and update `views/Dashboard.tsx`. For the active session, I'll add the layout with video player (auto-loop capable), timer/rep counter, and animated controls and add a translation dictionary: "Dashboard" to "Markas", "Profile" to "Profil Hero", and others. I'll add the necessary logic to `ActiveSession.tsx` to handle looped video segments.

payload.text: The user is experiencing errors with YouTube embeds (Error 153) and wants them fixed. Specifically, they want the video to display next to the counter during workouts. Please investigate the 'ActiveSession.tsx' and 'ExerciseModal.tsx' components for issues related to YouTube video embedding, particularly the `embedUrl` construction and iframe parameters. Ensure the embed works reliably and opens in a modal/side panel as intended. Also, try to implement a loop or continuous play for the video during the workout.

**Analyzing Embedding Issues**

I'm currently investigating the "Error 153" the user reported with the YouTube embed. It seems related to embedding permissions and the restrictions tied to that. I'm focusing on domain restrictions, non-embeddable videos, or potential URL parameter errors, especially with the "TIDAK BISA DI" phrase. I'm now cross-referencing embed documentation and the app's configuration to pinpoint the cause and suggest fixes.


**Addressing Language Support**

I'm now prioritizing language implementation. The code seems to have preliminary language selection with English and Indonesian, and translations are in place, so I'm investigating its functionality. The user wants "Indonesian santai," so I'll review how to refine the language setting to cater to that specific colloquial style, ensuring its seamless integration within the existing UI components.


**Enhancing Embed Integration**

I'm now prioritizing the user's need for a prominent YouTube embed, focusing on a workout tutorial adjacent to the counter. I'm expanding upon the existing player parameters (autoplay, mute, controls, loop, etc.) to investigate solutions to "Error 153", but also focus on ensuring proper display. I'll test diverse video types and, in parallel, I'll assess the best method for the integration of many animations to enrich the workout experience.


**Refining Embed Handling**

I'm now focusing on directly addressing the YouTube embed issue. I'm going to test replacing the existing embed code with the standard `youtube.com/embed` URL format to see if that resolves the "Error 153." I'll also confirm the video IDs in `data/exercises.ts` are not restricted; perhaps swapping them for popular fitness videos. Also, I will maintain the vertical split layout on mobile.


**Refining Animation Integration**

I'm now prioritizing the animation component, aiming for a richer workout experience. I'm exploring solutions for more intense animations to seamlessly integrate with the workout counter, ensuring a dynamic and engaging user interface. I also ensure that the Bahasa Indonesia translations will be refined for "santai". For the YouTube embed, I will look at the layout, ensuring that it is adjacent to the counter. I'm also reviewing `data/exercises.ts` and `ActiveSession.tsx` to update the video IFrame, including `playsinline=1`.


**Implementing Visual Feedback**

I'm now focusing on enriching the `ActiveSession.tsx` component with enhanced visual feedback. I'm exploring the implementation of a "Combo" counter, as well as particle effects triggered by user clicks to improve the interactivity. I'm refining the Bahasa Indonesia translations to enhance the "santai" tone. I've updated translations, replacing "Dashboard" with "Markas Utama", "Profile" with "Profil Lo", and others. Also, I'm refactoring the embed URL in `ActiveSession.tsx` to standardize the format, including `autoplay`, `mute`, and other parameters. I will also incorporate a potentially dynamic `origin` parameter for the YouTube embed.


**Verifying Embed URLs**

I'm now making final checks on embed URLs. I've updated the exercise video IDs in `data/exercises.ts` with generic, safe videos. I'm verifying the standard `youtube.com/embed` format, and will ensure `playsinline=1` is correctly set, regardless of the user's specific phrase. If "Cannot be played at all" persists, I'll switch back to `www.youtube.com`. I'm going to add CSS animations.


**Finalizing Integration Refinements**

I'm now focusing on integrating all fixes. I'm updating `data/translations.ts` to fully embrace the "santai" style. I'm also ensuring video IDs in `data/exercises.ts` use widely embeddable content for "Error 153" fix, utilizing Planet Fitness and Bowflex videos. Finally, I'm refactoring `ActiveSession.tsx` and `ExerciseModal.tsx`, and ensuring the layout matches the side-by-side design on larger screens, to address user's feedback.


**Implementing Visuals**

I'm now implementing the pulsing background on timer ticks and "shake" on button press. I'm updating `data/translations.ts` with "ID Santai" translations, for the next section. I will then review `data/exercises.ts` with better video IDs. I will update `views/ActiveSession.tsx` and `components/ExerciseModal.tsx`, fixing iframe logic, adding `playsinline=1` and ensuring "side by side" layout on desktop. I will be adding more visual effects.

payload.text:
**Analyzing the Error**

Okay, I'm currently focused on the error report. The problem stems from line 155 of `views/ActiveSession.tsx`, specifically the "Cannot find name 'Activity'" message. It appears the `Activity` type is undefined within the scope. I'm investigating the imports within the file to see if the type is missing or if there's a typo in its name or its path.


**Correcting the Import**

I've pinpointed the cause of the error: the `Activity` icon wasn't imported from `lucide-react` in `views/ActiveSession.tsx`. I need to add `Activity` to the import statement. After fixing this, I will check other files for potentially similar errors.


**Confirming the Fix**

I've confirmed the fix. I've updated `views/ActiveSession.tsx` to include `Activity` from `lucide-react`, resolving the import issue. The fix involves adding `Activity` to the import statement. Since the prompt only mentions this single file and error, I will not check any other files.


**Verifying the Inclusion**

I've reviewed the code in `views/ActiveSession.tsx` and have updated the imports to include the `Activity` icon from `lucide-react`. I've confirmed that the import statement now includes `Activity`. I've also verified the insertion of the `Activity` icon on line 155 of the file to ensure the correction addresses the original error. The XML generation is now complete.


**Refining the Component**

I'm now implementing the `ActiveSession` component. I've imported necessary dependencies: `React`, `useState`, `useEffect`, `useRef` from `react`, as well as `Exercise`, `ExerciseType`, and `Language` from `../types`. I've also incorporated components from the `../components/Shared` directory, and lucide-react icons, including the crucial `Activity` icon. My goal is to orchestrate the flow of an active exercise session, tracking progress and managing timers. I'm setting the props, and creating the initial component structure.


**Implementing the Timers**

I'm now integrating timer functionalities into the `ActiveSession` component. I've initialized state variables for a combo counter and translated text. I'm focusing on the `useEffect` hooks to manage both the global session timer and the exercise-specific timer. The exercise timer will count down based on the exercise type, while the session timer provides an overall duration.


**Managing the Set Progression**

I'm now working on the logic for progressing through sets and exercises within the component. I've focused on the `handleNextSet` function, which handles celebrations, set increments, exercise transitions and session completion. I'm ensuring smooth transitions and correct state updates for `currentIndex`, `currentSet`, and `isActive` based on the current exercise's characteristics and the timer. I've also added the function to call a completion handler.


**Displaying the Celebration**

I am now implementing the celebratory overlay. I'm dynamically displaying "MANTAP!" or "GREAT!" based on the selected language, which I've added to the component. I'm also ensuring the celebratory overlay is visually prominent, using animation and styling to make it eye-catching. Finally, I have optimized the embed URL to fix an issue.



**Adding Visual Effects**

I'm now focusing on enhancing the visual presentation. I've added a combo display with pulsing animation and subtle particle effects using CSS to make the interface more engaging. The `COMBO` display is now dynamic and responsive. Next, I'll integrate the video player, ensuring it has proper styling and responsiveness.



**Integrating the Video**

I'm now integrating the video player component within the ActiveSession. I've focused on displaying the exercise video dynamically, handling both online and offline states with appropriate fallback displays. I've incorporated a gradient overlay to improve text readability on top of the video and the necessary information overlay. The code structure for the `ActiveSession` component is now fully functional.



**Refining the UI**

I'm now refining the UI of the exercise display. I've focused on ensuring the text displays correctly over the video with a gradient overlay, and added a button to cancel the exercise session. I'm dynamically displaying the exercise name, the set number, and the translation in the correct language within the UI and adding the Activity icon.


**Updating the Timer**

I'm now integrating the header stats display. I'm focusing on incorporating the elapsed time and progress information. I'm dynamically displaying the formatted timer and the current exercise index within the UI. I'm styling these elements to match the overall design, ensuring a clear and concise presentation. I'm also ensuring the timer updates correctly.


**Finalizing the Component**

I'm now putting the finishing touches on the ActiveSession component. I'm focusing on ensuring the timer displays correctly. I am working on the progression bar and the styling, and the timers are now synchronized. I am refining the display of set information, the `Activity` icon is now inserted, and I'm ensuring everything aligns with the design.


**Refining the Timer**

I'm now incorporating pause/play functionality to the timer, and integrating progress tracking. I'm dynamically displaying the formatted time, current set, and set information. I've focused on styling and animation of the timer to match the overall design, ensuring a clean and concise presentation. I'm also now adding the progression bar.


**Integrating the Button**

I'm now integrating the rotate button. I've focused on ensuring the button rotates correctly on click, and triggers a new function. This will reset the timer, or perform any other actions related to the exercise. I've also incorporated visual feedback for the button state, such as hover effects and a shadow to improve the user experience.



**Implementing the Button**

I'm now integrating the primary button, including both "Complete Set" and "Complete Quest" variations. The design incorporates visual effects like scaling on hover and active states, as well as a subtle white overlay animation. I'm dynamically updating the button text and incorporating either a checkmark or trophy icon depending on the current exercise's state. I've also incorporated a shadow on the button.
